quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Addition rule (general)"?

options.text: The magnitude of the difference between the true parameter and the null value; determines the practical importance of a statistically significant result
V = √(χ²/(n·min(r-1, c-1))); ranges from 0 to 1, measuring the strength of association between two categorical variables
P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B); applies to any two events, subtracting the overlap to avoid double-counting
Tests H₀: μ = μ₀ using t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n) with df = n - 1; used when σ is unknown

quiz_questions.explanation: "Addition rule (general)" is defined as: P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B); applies to any two events, subtracting the overlap to avoid double-counting. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Mutually exclusive events"?

options.text: μ = np; the expected number of successes in n trials
A scatterplot of residuals versus the explanatory variable; used to check whether a linear model is appropriate for the data
Events that cannot occur simultaneously; P(A and B) = 0, so P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)
The probability of obtaining a test statistic as extreme as or more extreme than the observed value, assuming H₀ is true

quiz_questions.explanation: "Mutually exclusive events" is defined as: Events that cannot occur simultaneously; P(A and B) = 0, so P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Multiplication rule (general)"?

options.text: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A); the probability of both events equals the probability of the first times the conditional probability of the second
Failing to reject H₀ when Hₐ is actually true; probability denoted β
A scatterplot of residuals versus the explanatory variable; used to check whether a linear model is appropriate for the data
A table giving cumulative probabilities (areas to the left) for z-scores in the standard normal distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: "Multiplication rule (general)" is defined as: P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A); the probability of both events equals the probability of the first times the conditional probability of the second. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Complement"?

options.text: After forming blocks, using a chance mechanism to assign treatments within each block to balance remaining confounders
n = (t*·s/ME)²; the sample size needed to achieve a desired margin of error, often requiring an initial pilot study to estimate s
The long-run proportion of intervals that capture the true parameter; e.g., 95% means 95 out of 100 intervals would contain p
The complement of event A (Aᶜ) consists of all outcomes not in A; P(Aᶜ) = 1 - P(A)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Complement" is defined as: The complement of event A (Aᶜ) consists of all outcomes not in A; P(Aᶜ) = 1 - P(A). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Union and intersection"?

options.text: A table giving cumulative probabilities (areas to the left) for z-scores in the standard normal distribution
An experimental design where units are grouped into blocks by a confounding variable, then treatments are randomly assigned within each block
A ∪ B ("A or B") includes outcomes in either event; A ∩ B ("A and B") includes only outcomes in both events
The fraction of successes in a sample: p̂ = X/n, where X is the number of successes

quiz_questions.explanation: "Union and intersection" is defined as: A ∪ B ("A or B") includes outcomes in either event; A ∩ B ("A and B") includes only outcomes in both events. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B); applies to any two events, subtracting the overlap to avoid double-counting"?

options.text: Percentile
Addition rule (general)
Quantitative variable
Two-way table

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Addition rule (general)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Events that cannot occur simultaneously; P(A and B) = 0, so P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)"?

options.text: Significance level (α)
Mutually exclusive events
Margin of error (for means)
Interpretation of slope CI

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Mutually exclusive events".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B|A); the probability of both events equals the probability of the first times the conditional probability of the second"?

options.text: Multiplication rule (general)
Placebo
Replication
Standardized residual

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Multiplication rule (general)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The complement of event A (Aᶜ) consists of all outcomes not in A; P(Aᶜ) = 1 - P(A)"?

options.text: Complement
Marginal distribution
Simpson's Paradox
Test statistic

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Complement".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A ∪ B ("A or B") includes outcomes in either event; A ∩ B ("A and B") includes only outcomes in both events"?

options.text: Interpretation of slope CI
Union and intersection
Test statistic
Simpson's Paradox

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Union and intersection".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Basic Probability Rules is accurate?

options.text: Comparing Two Proportions tests whether two population proportions differ using the test statistic z = (p̂₁ - p̂₂)/√(p̂c(1-p̂c)(1/n₁ + 1/n₂)), where p̂c is the pooled proportion under H₀: p₁ = p₂.
If the population has mean μ and standard deviation σ, then the sampling distribution of x̄ has mean μx̄ = μ and standard deviation σx̄ = σ/√n (the standard error).

The three principles of experimental design are control (using comparison groups), randomization (assigning treatments randomly to reduce bias), and replication (using enough subjects to detect real effects).
Basic Probability Rules provide the mathematical framework for calculating probabilities of compound events.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Basic Probability Rules as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Basic Probability Rules?

options.text: Two-way tables (contingency tables) organize counts for two categorical variables and allow calculation of marginal, joint, and conditional distributions.
Range, IQR, and standard deviation each capture different aspects of spread, and choosing the right measure depends on the data's shape and the presence of outliers.

The binomial distribution counts the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials with constant success probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), with mean np and standard deviation √(np(1-p)).

The addition rule for mutually exclusive events (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)) and the general addition rule (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)) handle unions, while the multiplication rule handles intersections.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Basic Probability Rules as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Basic Probability Rules?

options.text: Conditional Probability measures the probability of an event given that another event has occurred: P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).
Technology output typically provides b₁, SE(b₁), t, and p-value directly, so interpreting computer regression output is an essential skill.
Data collection methods—including census, survey, and experiment—each have distinct strengths and limitations that affect the validity and generalizability of conclusions.
These rules, combined with complement and conditional probability, enable calculation of any probability from basic event probabilities.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Basic Probability Rules as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Basic Probability Rules?

options.text: Hypotheses and P-Values form the logical framework for statistical significance testing.
A randomized block design combines the benefits of both techniques: blocking controls for known confounders while randomization balances unknown confounders across treatment groups.
Basic Probability Rules provide the mathematical framework for calculating probabilities of compound events.
The CLT also applies to sample proportions and sums.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Basic Probability Rules as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Basic Probability Rules?

options.text: Model comparison using r² and residual plots helps determine which model (linear, exponential, power) best fits the data.

The addition rule for mutually exclusive events (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)) and the general addition rule (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)) handle unions, while the multiplication rule handles intersections.
A randomized block design combines the benefits of both techniques: blocking controls for known confounders while randomization balances unknown confounders across treatment groups.
The interval provides a range of plausible values for the rate of change in y per unit change in x.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Basic Probability Rules as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Basic Probability Rules?

options.text: Market research testing whether product preference is associated with age group to determine whether different demographics need different marketing strategies
Calculating the probability of drawing a heart or a face card from a standard deck using the general addition rule (accounting for hearts that are face cards)
Quality control in manufacturing using μ ± 3σ control limits to detect when a production process drifts out of specification
Taste tests where each consumer rates two products, analyzing the paired differences to determine if there is a preference for one product over the other

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Basic Probability Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Basic Probability Rules?

options.text: Medical screening: computing the probability of getting at least one false positive in multiple independent tests using the complement rule: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
Environmental agency testing whether mean mercury concentration in fish exceeds the 0.5 ppm safety limit (H₀: μ = 0.5, Hₐ: μ > 0.5) to issue consumption advisories
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational
Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Basic Probability Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Basic Probability Rules applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Risk assessment in engineering using multiplication rules to calculate the probability that multiple independent safety systems all fail simultaneously
Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits
Medical research blocking patients by disease severity before randomly assigning drug vs. placebo within each severity block
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Basic Probability Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Basic Probability Rules?

options.text: Calculating the probability of drawing a heart or a face card from a standard deck using the general addition rule (accounting for hearts that are face cards)
Taste tests where each consumer rates two products, analyzing the paired differences to determine if there is a preference for one product over the other
Manufacturing process monitoring using the sampling distribution of x̄ to set control chart limits for detecting when a process mean shifts
College admissions data examining acceptance rates conditional on gender to investigate potential bias, while being alert for Simpson's Paradox across departments

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Basic Probability Rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Basic Probability Rules in practice?

options.text: Climate scientists testing whether year is a significant predictor of global temperature anomaly (testing for a statistically significant warming trend)
Healthcare studies using cluster sampling of hospitals rather than individual patients when a complete patient list is impractical
Medical screening: computing the probability of getting at least one false positive in multiple independent tests using the complement rule: P(at least one) = 1 - P(none)
Quality control using CLT to construct control charts for process means even when individual measurements follow a skewed distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Basic Probability Rules.
